executive_summary: IGIS is a very small, expertise-heavy statutory oversight agency that provides independent assurance on the legality and propriety of Australia's six intelligence agencies (plus network-activity-warrant oversight of ACIC and AFP). Its 2025-26 plan is dominated by three pressures: a likely expansion of its jurisdiction (ISLAB and the agreed recommendation to bring all of ACIC under IGIS), acute recruitment and retention difficulties compounded by lengthy security-clearance timeframes, and the need to lift technical/AI literacy to keep pace with the agencies it oversees. Note the plan sits in the Attorney-General's portfolio per the cooperation section, despite the brief's PM&C label, and IGIS depends on shared services from the Attorney-General's Department. The addressable consulting market is genuinely narrow: core oversight work is done only by cleared APS officers, and high-clearance constraints limit external delivery. The most credible BD entry points are corporate/enabling functions — workforce strategy, information governance, risk-framework uplift and operating-model design for an expanding remit — kept proportionate to a small-agency budget.

strategic_priorities.title: Sustain rigorous oversight amid an expanding intelligence community
strategic_priorities.description: IGIS's primary focus is effective oversight in an environment of growing agency size, high intelligence tempo and technological change, while optimising its own activities to remain focused, efficient and proportionate.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: Our primary focus remains on effective oversight of the intelligence agencies ... in an environment of an expanding intelligence community, a high tempo of intelligence effort and the complex implications of technological change.

strategic_priorities.title: Prepare for an expanded jurisdiction
strategic_priorities.description: The lapsed ISLAB and the government's agreed recommendation to have IGIS oversee the entirety of ACIC (in lieu of the Commonwealth Ombudsman) signal a coming expansion of remit that will drive activity and require new capacity and processes.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: the proposed expansion of our jurisdiction should the government continue its plan to include additional agencies under our remit, will be a key driver of activity in the Office over 2025-26.

strategic_priorities.title: Recruit and retain specialised, security-cleared workforce
strategic_priorities.description: IGIS is implementing new and innovative HR strategies, an Employee Value Proposition, talent acquisition and succession planning to address acute skills shortages and clearance-related onboarding delays.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: we will continue to manage the challenges and opportunities of recruiting and retaining a high-quality workforce.

strategic_priorities.title: Build technical and AI literacy for oversight
strategic_priorities.description: Advances in technology and increasing AI use by overseen agencies require IGIS to develop staff technical literacy and ensure technical expertise (currently via a seconded Technical Advisor) is available to inform oversight.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: The IGIS is continuing to develop the technical literacy of staff and to ensure technical expertise is available to inform our oversight activities.

strategic_priorities.title: Handle increasingly complex complaints and PIDs
strategic_priorities.description: Complaint volumes are stable but newer complaints from current/former staff demand more resources and require managing the complex interaction between the IGIS Act and the PID Act.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: more recently received complaints are typically requiring significantly more resources and expertise to investigate

reform_and_change_drivers: Intelligence Services Legislation Amendment Bill 2023 (ISLAB) lapsed at prorogation; potential reintroduction would significantly expand IGIS jurisdiction to ACIC, AUSTRAC intelligence functions, AFP and Home Affairs intelligence functions
Government's November 2024 agreement to have IGIS oversee the entirety of ACIC (replacing the Commonwealth Ombudsman) following the independent review of the ACIC
2024 Independent Intelligence Review affirming the oversight model as fit for purpose but noting continued growth of the intelligence community
Proposed reforms to the Public Interest Disclosure Act 2013 and the need to manage IGIS Act / PID Act interactions
Significant investment and growth in capacity and capability across the overseen intelligence agencies, increasing oversight workload
Increasing use of artificial intelligence and advanced technology by overseen agencies
Anticipated growth in strategic risks driven by an expanding workforce and evolving jurisdiction
challenges_and_risks: Persistent recruitment and retention difficulties in a tight national-security labour market with strong competition for cyber, intelligence and strategic-policy talent
Lengthy external security-clearance processes causing delays between selection and commencement of staff
Small-agency vulnerability: loss of key, experienced staff poses outsized risk
Rising complexity and resource intensity of complaints, particularly from current/former intelligence staff
Maintaining sufficient technical/AI literacy to credibly oversee technologically advanced agencies
Strategic risks expected to grow with expanding workforce and jurisdiction
Dependence on shared-services arrangements with the Attorney-General's Department for ICT mobility and some systems
Bringing records and information governance into line with National Archives / Commonwealth requirements across electronic and hardcopy holdings
Qualitative-only performance framework limits demonstrable, quantitative evidence of effectiveness

opportunity_areas.area: Workforce attraction, retention and EVP refinement
opportunity_areas.agency_need: Acute skills shortages, clearance-driven onboarding delays and high impact of losing key staff in a small agency; commitment to refine the Employee Value Proposition and develop a Census 2024 action plan.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: A right-sized workforce planning and retention review: refresh the EVP, map critical-role succession and clearance-pipeline risk, and translate APS Census 2024 results into a practical engagement/retention action plan.
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan explicitly names recruitment/retention as a multi-year challenge, lists discrete HR strategies (EVP, talent acquisition, succession, Census action plan), and frames this as an enabling priority — typically the easiest advisory work to win and well-suited to a small budget.

opportunity_areas.area: Operating-model design for an expanding jurisdiction
opportunity_areas.agency_need: Likely absorption of full ACIC oversight (and potentially AUSTRAC/AFP/Home Affairs intelligence functions) requires IGIS to scale processes, resourcing and structure proportionately.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: A target operating model / capacity-planning piece scoping how IGIS would resource, structure and sequence the take-on of expanded oversight responsibilities, with options proportionate to phased legislative change.
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan repeatedly flags jurisdiction expansion as the key driver of activity and the government has agreed to the ACIC oversight transfer; planning demand is real, though timing depends on legislation being reintroduced.

opportunity_areas.area: Risk framework uplift for a growing risk profile
opportunity_areas.agency_need: IGIS anticipates strategic risks will grow with an expanding workforce, evolving jurisdiction and changing national-security environment.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: A short risk-framework refresh aligning the Risk Management Policy, register and appetite statements to the expanded-jurisdiction scenario, with refreshed strategic-risk assessments and owner mapping.
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan states strategic risks 'will grow' over the period and details an existing risk framework, register and related plans — a clear, bounded uplift opportunity.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Ongoing work to update information architecture and bring recordkeeping into line with National Archives and Commonwealth information-management policies across electronic and hardcopy holdings.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: An information strategy / information-management roadmap covering records architecture, digitisation prioritisation and compliance with National Archives requirements — scoped for a small, highly classified environment.
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan names an Information Governance Framework with explicit plans to update architecture and transition recordkeeping to digital, while acknowledging some records cannot be digitised — a defined, proportionate engagement.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Staff depend on physical office/systems access; IGIS wants to identify tasks performable remotely or in lower-classification workspaces, but relies on a shared-services remote-working platform via the Attorney-General's Department.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: A business/process analysis of which oversight and corporate tasks can be safely performed in lower-classification or mobile settings, with secure-by-design service options feeding the shared-services arrangement.
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan flags a deliberate intent to expand officer mobility within security limits; however, the shared-services dependency on AGD and clearance constraints materially narrow what an external firm can deliver.

recommended_actions.action: Approach the IGIS corporate/HR function (and accountable authority's office) with a concise workforce retention and EVP capability statement tailored to small, high-clearance agencies.
recommended_actions.rationale: Recruitment/retention is the most explicitly and repeatedly stated enabling challenge in the plan, is politically low-sensitivity, and suits a short, proportionate engagement a small agency can fund.

recommended_actions.action: Prepare a short thought piece on operating-model and capacity options for oversight bodies facing jurisdiction expansion, referencing the agreed ACIC oversight transfer, and offer a scoping conversation.
recommended_actions.rationale: Positions the firm ahead of any ISLAB reintroduction; demand is contingent on legislation so leading with insight rather than a hard pitch is appropriate.

recommended_actions.action: Monitor reintroduction of ISLAB and progress of the ACIC oversight transfer through PJCIS and the Attorney-General's portfolio as a trigger for capacity, risk and operating-model work.
recommended_actions.rationale: These legislative changes are the single largest future driver of IGIS activity and would convert latent demand into funded work.

recommended_actions.action: Offer a bounded risk-framework refresh aligned to the expanded-jurisdiction scenario to the Chief Risk / governance function.
recommended_actions.rationale: The plan states strategic risks will grow; a small, defined review is credible but lower-urgency than workforce work.

engagement_targets: Accountable authority's office (Inspector-General) — via senior corporate leadership rather than direct, given the agency's size
Head of corporate / People & HR function (workforce, EVP, Census action plan)
Governance and risk function / risk owner (Risk Management Framework)
Liaison points with the Attorney-General's Department shared-services arrangement (ICT mobility, systems)
talking_points: IGIS's expanding remit — particularly the agreed transfer of full ACIC oversight — will require scaling processes, resourcing and structure; we can help scope a proportionate operating model before the workload arrives.

Recruitment and retention in a tight, high-clearance market is your stated multi-year challenge; we can refresh your EVP and turn Census 2024 results into a practical retention action plan sized for a small agency.
As your strategic risk profile grows with workforce and jurisdiction, a light-touch risk-framework refresh can keep appetite, register and assessments aligned to the expanded scenario.
Your information-governance roadmap and recordkeeping digitisation can be advanced with an information strategy designed for a highly classified environment and National Archives compliance.
We understand small oversight agencies and the clearance/secrecy constraints that shape what external partners can and cannot do — our offerings are scoped accordingly.
caveats: This is a small, security-constrained oversight agency with a deliberately qualitative performance framework and almost no public procurement footprint. Core business is done only by cleared APS officers, and high security-clearance requirements plus an existing shared-services dependency on the Attorney-General's Department materially limit what an external firm can deliver. All opportunities sit in enabling/corporate functions and are modest in scale; none maps to a named, funded program. The most significant future driver (jurisdiction expansion via ISLAB / ACIC oversight transfer) is contingent on legislation that lapsed at prorogation. External research returned no verifiable news, ANAO or parliamentary findings, so those sections are empty by design rather than oversight. Note also an apparent portfolio discrepancy — the plan's cooperation section places IGIS within the Attorney-General's portfolio, not PM&C as labelled.
